title: NIB in stealth restructure, defence mandate change-up
full_text:
In the weeks after offloading its travel division to Allianz Partners, NIB Holdings is on the move again.
Street Talk understands the $3.4 billion health insurance giant has kicked off a significant internal restructure, yet to be disclosed to the market, which will roll out over the next two months. This has already seen the departure of long-serving chief executive of the private health insurance division, James Barr, in recent weeks.
Ed Close, CEO and MD of NIB Holdings, has been in the top job since December 2024. Michael Quelch
Barr was appointed chief of the group’s flagship health insurance business in 2024, sitting below chief executive Ed Close and chief financial officer Nick Freeman in the senior management team. He first joined NIB in 2005 and has held senior management roles in marketing and product. Close has been in the top job since December 2024, succeeding Mark Fitzgibbon.
Sources said Barr’s exit after more than 20 years also saw other senior members in the division let go. NIB made a passing mention of a group-wide productivity program and related restructure in December, but has been quiet on the exits this month.
A spokesperson for NIB said the insurer “regularly reviews” its operating model but wouldn’t comment on the employment status of individuals.
“NIB regularly reviews our operating model to ensure it is aligned to our corporate strategy, including ensuring our organisational structure is fit for purpose, however, we don’t comment on any individual employment matters.”
Street Talk can also reveal NIB has switched up its defence mandate to Morgan Stanley from Citi in a nod to the strength of the Wall Street bank’s healthcare, financial institutions and defence platform. Jarden advised NIB on the $50 million sale of a large portion of its Australian and New Zealand travel distribution portfolio, announced this month.
‘Cautious outlook’
Morgan Stanley is currently advising private equity firm Crescent Capital Partners on the sale of National Dental Care, one of Australia’s largest dental networks, acted for Bupa on its acquisition of Quadrant Private Equity’s medical centres business Partnered Health and is adviser to Brookfield on its exit efforts at La Trobe Financial.
Its healthcare lead, managing director Rowan Bailey, was promoted to co-lead a broader diversified industries group alongside Andrew Hunter in June.
The NIB spokesperson confirmed Morgan Stanley’s appointment, saying: “NIB has established relationships with a range of corporate advisory firms since its listing on the ASX since 2007.
“On a regular basis, we test the market to ensure these firms are continuing to meet NIB’s best interests and strategy. As part of that process, we undertook a market assessment and recently, appointed Morgan Stanley as our corporate defence advisory firm.”
In a May note, Macquarie’s research division said it expects NIB to underperform and retain a “cautious outlook” with “multiple divisions experiencing operational and environmental headwinds”. Shares in the company have been flat this year, as was its $82.9 million first-half net profit after tax, coming in line with expectations and flat on the previous corresponding period.
NIB’s core is its Australian private health insurance business with about 10 per cent market share. It also has products for international students and workers, and is the second-largest private health insurance provider in New Zealand. NIB also operates an NDIS plan-management brand, and a range of health services and insurance partnerships.

heading: Health and Disability Insurance
